transcript.transcript: Interview with Mrs. Molly Bennett, November 28th, 2000. Mrs. Bennett, can you tell me about your generational links to the West Indies? Well, my mother and father is from Jamaica. They were born there. And I am an offspring of Lester Big State and Ethel Amanda Wilson, the first of 12 children. Where were you born? I was born in Lower Saint Andrew in Jamaica. What year? The year was February 18. 1935. Do you have any particular memories of growing up in Saint Andrew? I have fond memories of growing up. I was fortunate to have parents that had their own home, and as a girl we had a lot of fruit trees and flowers. We were situated on two lots of land. The house was on one. One lot was emptied, which we manicured with beautiful plants, and my mother reared a lot of goats and birds, and we had a lot of fowls and turkey in those days. Turkey, turkey and the turkey was kept until Christmas, when we do rear them at the end of the year to meet the Christmas market. There was people that would use turkey back in those days. And I'm talking in the early 50s and the 50s. At the time when you were growing up, uh, in your community in Saint Andrew, did you know of anybody who was going overseas? A lot of people want some of the people that I can recall had gone to Liberia. In Africa, a group of people from in the Maxwell Park area and in half a tree here that had gone to Liberia. And in my family, my grand uncle went to England from 1914. Do you know how he got a chance to go to England in that early period? Well, they had you know, they're really a traveling family. My family. And he had gone there from the war and met his wife and had children so migrated to Australia. And when I had the chance to go to England in 1952, I stayed at his home. And he was very much well and alive and working at the Pentonville Post Office. What's it called? Pen. Pentonville. I think it's in the north side of England, in Kings Cross. The post office that he works. So your uncle stayed in England permanently? Yes, yes. And how did you arrange to go to England in 1952. I went to England because I went to pursue a nursing career, which I did, and I went to London to him, and then I went down to Richmond, to Hounslow, to the nursing school where I did three years. Um. And how how long was your entire time in England? I spent five years. And did any particular events, uh, stand out as a, you know, young Jamaican woman going overseas for the first time? Yes. Um, the very first thing they had the, um, coronation of the Queen in 1952. And I had gone to witness that. I went outside the gate of Buckingham Palace, uh, along with millions of other people. And I saw when she came out and the balcony and wave as the people shouted, we want the queen, we want the queen, you know. And, um, I went on to go to all the bright spots of London, the Hammersmith Palais, which was the place to be when you're growing up, to go for formal ballroom dancing and, um, Covent Gardens, we would get ticket to go to Covent Gardens and that's another big place, opera place that would have a chance to go when you're young, coming out of Jamaica, you'd go to all those nice places. Did you have any bad experiences while you were in England? No. No, no. Old people may have it, but I was always more or less fortunate to be surrounded with nice, wonderful people. I went there just after the war, and when I was a student nurse, the patient would call me darky and I said, darky, I save you some sugar, and sugar was rationed and they would have a little tin quarter pound of sugar. They would save it for me. We need to get in a candy. You'd have to go outside to the matron's office by nine to get the coupon. Because your coupon. She kept the coupon for sugar, meat, tea and all those things. And coming from the West Indies, where we have a lot of sugar to, like, actually learn to drink tea without sugar. But these people took me in so wonderfully they would knit me scarves, sweaters. So I have no bad reflection, you know, to say that I did not meet good people at that time. You weren't offended to be called darkie? No, I was not. Because they calling me darkie was out of love. They would say darkie when I'm in the outpatient darkie. If I come tomorrow morning. Will you wait on me early? I said yes. And they said I'm going to Shepherd's Bush. Shepherd's Bush is where we go shopping. Or I'm going up to King's Cross. And by Monday morning, you bet they would bring me stockings. Anything. Any little gifts that would be bringing back for me. So we had a good relationship both with the nursing people, the administration and the patients. The relationship was just wonderful. What kind of visa did you have to go to England. At that time? We didn't need a visa. My mother sent me on a luxury liner. Everyone was first class. Those days it was 100 sterling pounds. At that time, my mother could have bought five lots of land in Jamaica. Five lots. And she invested £100 in my first year. Everyone on that ship was first class going to London. How did your mum come to be so well off? My mother, when the first place we had our own home, and in the second place, my mother would buy a house and fix them and sell them back. And we make money. And my father was a very good cabinet maker and he was also a carpenter. So we have the knowledge, the expertise, so to speak. So we plus my mother would, you know, would be a she would sell material cloth and all of that. So we were very well self-contained in making money, making money from we would have, as I tell you about we would raise fowl and turkey would have goats. So there's always something that we would have to sell. So at that time, um, between. Between the time your uncle went to England and the time that you went to England. As long as you had the money, you could go right? Right. Okay. And what occasion you're leaving England after five years. Well, I came to United States. Here. My husband came here to America in 1951, and I went there after to England in 52. Can you say a little bit about how you met your husband in Jamaica? My husband grew up on the same street. He's from Stonehill. But when they came to go to school, they bought a house downtown where we were. When I say in Lower Saint Andrew below halfway tree. Because this way. Yeah. My husband is from Stony Hill, which is about ten miles from where we are. So they had bought a house down the halfway tree where we were, and then he grew up there and attend high school and all of that. And when did you first know that the two of you were We're going to end up together. We didn't know that. We just grew up as kids. His mother was a very good friend of my mother. And then his sisters. Um, and I was very good friends. So after we traveled and we correspond, we all get back together. I come, he came here, then I came here after because he came here first in 51. I went to England. So by 57, I find myself here. See what, uh, brought Colin Bennett here in 1951. Well, what brought my husband here those days? You could not get the opportunity to come to America from the West Indies. Because they had a quota system, which only allowed you 100% to come from the West Indies. So his only chance of getting here to the Americas was through the farm work. He was bright, ambitious, but there was no way of getting there but through the farm. So he came and did a magnificent job working, but never lose focus of his dreams and goals. Do you know which farm he worked in? Um, I remember them. They are in Windsor. Uh, I've heard the name, and, um, one of the man I hear him talking about was Mr. Kravis. Kravis, I think. And it was quite a few of them up there. How long did your husband work in? In the farm. In farm labor. Well. I would imagine a couple of years, I would imagine a couple of years. So what brought you here in 1957, exactly? Well, I came because he was here. But you weren't together yet? No, but we always correspond. We always correspond. So did you come because of a romantic interest that you had in him or. Or you came because you you always wanted to come to America? No, I for me, I didn't come because I wanted to come to America. When I finish my nurses in nursing in England and reach back to Jamaica. My father was very disappointed that I was coming out back and not staying in Jamaica. And I promised them that I would only stay there for five. I would only come back and stay here for five years and come back home. Actually my father, we built our house larger so I could be there. And my father made my furniture and all of that. And I said, five years I would come and stay here, and then five years become ten, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 until he passed away. But we came and we go to school. He went to school. I went to school here, and then he went into business. And I gave up all my ambition and work with him in the business. That is what we now have here. How did you come to settle down in the Hartford area? Because he was here. Oh, I understand that, but, I mean, we're in the Hartford area. Did you live right here? I tell you, when my husband called for an apartment those days, they thought he was an Englishman. And they would say to him, come for the apartment. And when he showed up and they saw that his pigmentation was different. I choose the word pigmentation. It was different. He could not get it. And some of those streets, like I will tell you, Capen Street. Vine Street is those are not street of choice anymore. And he would go there and he would not get it. But he had a lawyer friend and a lawyer friend told him where a house was that he could buy. When we got there was a two family. If I should jump on the floor, the the front porch, it would collapse. And we bought that house for 12,500 those days, and we put down 500 and the man gave us back the mortgage. I want you to know, I used to buy coal by the truckload from Savin brother. My husband had signed us so he could not clean the cinder every day. Every day I would go down the basement and clean out all that cinder. That is the coal that burn. Clean it out, pack back the stove, light it. I was working then as a nurse assistant and had three, two, 11 and having kids, and I had to clean that furnace every day. It was for a two family house. Then. God help us. And we change from coal and we put in an oil furnace. We put siding on the building we actually built over the building. And the dump was a wonderful place for us. The dump, the dump, the dump that things used to be disregarded and they don't know. It becomes that place over the meadows. They call it the Meadows, where it's a world to itself, where you have the theater, you have car sales, you have the post office. Those days I went over to the Meadows, the dump, and I pick up all the brick that I could and put a fence. The fence is about 120ft or one 20ft, 25ft in length. The fence is still standing today. We pick up all those bricks at the dump for free and made a wall a fence, because all my dirt was watching over to the next yard. So we have come a far way. But we have. We know about houses we know to tear down and build. We know what to do so because we know what to do and because we know what we want, it wasn't hard for us to implement it and put it in place. Even that fence that is standing there today after 40 odd years, the first house we bought. Uh, where was this house? What street? Elmer Street, 22 Elmer Street. And where was Mr. Bennett living before you finally got 22 Elmer Street? Well, those days, okay. When we first come, we could not live together because there was a thing called love marriage. Or get the proper pronunciation and spelling. If you ever live with a man, the police would be right there on your door to lock you up. And you would never, never even trust your best friend to let you know you're sleeping with a man and he's not married because you can't trust anybody. They would send the police for you at night. What year was this? This was way back in the 50s, early 50s. And coming up, there was a policewoman, American. She. She was good at it. This this this police woman that she had, she was number one for taking in the boys that did these things sleeping. And it would be in the papers. You could not sleep with a woman unless vice versa. Woman couldn't sleep with man because you would be arrested. So where did you end up? So he was living in one place and I was living. Not until we were married that we lived together. So where were you living? Right here in Hartford. No, I'm trying to get a sense of where people live before they finally got their first house. What street do you live? Um, I was at Enfield Street and he was at Clark Street. At apartments? Yeah, I live at Enfield. He lived at Clark. And when we bought the house, we live at Elmer. And how long did you two stay at Elmer? Oh, All, we stayed at Helmar and I had the third child, which was barren, and my husband decided to buy the house in the height of winter. We only had a two bedroom, and we were going up to a three bedroom living and dining downstairs, and three bedroom upstairs with a finished basement in Tower Avenue, and I couldn't tell you what the lawn looked like or anything, because he bought it when there was about 6 to 8in of snow on the ground. But the house was vacant and it was so beautiful. It has a fireplace. It was a brick house, beautiful cabinets. The man was a veteran and he had built a house in Rocky Hill and we bought it. And. Um, how did Hartford compare to London when you you came here and settled down? Well, completely a different lifestyle. So in in England, you don't have a Frigidaire. So what? It's not the end of the world. The green grocer would come. He would put the loaf of bread at the step without any paper. The dog would come, maybe, and smell it. So what? So you want something for your dinner? And you go to the greengrocer and you buy what you want. You come and you cook, and tomorrow you do the same thing. Because what? You don't have a fridge and you put your milk out on the ledge, the window ledge. Life was still beautiful. Now that you don't know any better. And I want to tell you, this was in the heart of London. If you know London and you know Eustace Houston, You used to used to the station and, you know, King's Cross. I was living at Argyle Street and we have Temple Bar and all the place of learning. You were, you know, they say you're called to the inner Court, go to Temple Bar and all those place grazing. I was right there, nearby. So when I was living up in London and my uncle lived in King's Cross. So, you know, that was the style. And then when you come here now, you have, you know, what was interesting with London. You don't want to believe it. Here it is. I come from a place of sugar. I come from a place where you have rivers. You have the sea. You can bathe 2 or 3 times a day if you wish. When you go to London, they don't have any bathhouses. Some of the places, trust me. And you go to the bathhouse for a bath every Sunday, once a week. The average people person. Bartos, you heard of that? Okay. Have you been well? I think it changed. I used to go up to Kings Cross and you go to the Bartos and you pay and you bathe. The weekend was mostly like when you go to bed. I suppose you can't go during the time the days to all the time. But you go. And that was it. Life goes on. So how was Hartford different? When you come, you have everything here. You have Frigidaire. You have you can bathe. You have, you know, just like similar to home, unless you have in the winter months, you know. And what about the community of of people in Hartford? I mean, were there a lot of West Indians in London compared to Hartford for you? Well, at that time you would go to find you. When I was down in Richmond, I would come all the way up to Brixton, and at Brixton you would find some Allerton Road, you would find some of your own people. And that's where I used to come and have my hair done and be with, you know, some of my own. Um, here in America, in Hartford. You would know anytime a Jamaican come because everybody would be so happy that you would say, you know, it's three of us here today. You know, it's four of us. You know, it's six of us. So everybody when I knew Jamaican girl come out so everybody would be excited. Go check her out. Embrace her. You know, those days, so to speak. So it was closely knit. Okay. Where did you shop. Girl. We had a lot of shops. We had a lot of shops. We have Sage-allen. We have G. Fox's. This was the Mecca because those days you did not have the mall as you have today. And that week the inner city. But at any given time you could go to the big stores Wars and there would be a slot for you. And what what kind of West Indian businesses I actually meant were around like none. None. None of us know. So where where did you get green banana and yam? That was to come if we were to get those earlier days. It came from New York, you know, until afterward, other entrepreneur went and start to set up shops. One of the men who set up shop is now dead was Mr. Leslie, that he had controlled the market for a long time by going out to New York and bringing back the the product. What was the name of his store? Leslie General Store that was on Barbara Street. He died about a year or two ago. And that was just a general supermarket? No, just a store. Not a, you know, it was corner store, you know, but he carried the yam and cocoa and everything West Indian. But before that, um, we'd get the things from New York. And if anybody going down, they would buy enough and bring it back. You know, they'd get some banana and anything. And where did you get Patty? People would make if anybody know what to do it, they would do it. There was a man that called him a man. He would make some. And you know, if he made it like he was making a batch, you'd say, save me two, you know, that sort of thing. Uh, well, you know, remember. Mr. Reed, I think his wife died. I don't know if he's still around. I don't know, I haven't seen, uh. I haven't seen him in a long, long time. Where was where where was he living? He used to live on Cleveland Avenue, but I don't know where he is. You know, when you look around, sometimes people get old and in convalescent home and all of that. I don't know where he is. So what were when was the first bakery and patty shop that you can recall? The first one, when Mrs. Scott came to town, he came and said, Mr. Bennett, my husband and my husband here recommended a place to him. The building is now torn down. And after that he went down to the city and able to purchase land across the street in Albany Avenue and put up the bakery across the street. And that maybe go back about ten, ten time go by quickly, at least ten years or so. Ten years ago. If not more. You know about the facts, really. Mr.. Scott? Scott baker. Mhm. And, um, can you tell me how your husband came to be involved with the insurance and real estate business? Well, you know, my husband was a politician. In, he served as Republican. Um, he won four consecutive two year term on the City of Hartford. City of Hartford. And, um. When we were younger, growing up, we had a large hall on our street, Galloway Road. There was a large hall and. Politician used to come there. Madam Rosaleen Iris King. As a matter of fact, the first Prime Minister of Jamaica. Bustamante. Bustamante lived couple blocks up the street from where our house is. And Bustamante. At that time we used to have cows at his house. You would see him milking it. And when him chauffeur driving him to town because we used to come to school downtown. We would get drive in this big, lovely car and would say, come on, my children, come on. And he would take up a few of us and give us a ride downtown when we were kids. And then we have LG Newlon. He was labor. LG Newlon live up the street a couple blocks away from us. Then you have you Shearer. You Shearer used to live on Chisholm Avenue, which they all you Shearer, Newlon and Buster they all live. Shearer is Chisholm Avenue on the opposite side is is is um on a boy newlon and then expect avenue was was Buster afterward. Buster lived from Durango live in Tucker avenue over Mountain View. And so from we were growing up we were seeing these giants, political people, you understand? And it's because of the involvement with the political people, why my husband was able to get even a ticket to come here from Madame Rose. Leon. So politics. My hunting was the first MHR in Saint Thomas to be PMP, because in Saint Thomas is Labor stronghold and Violet Thompson, she's still alive, but she's very old now. My auntie was MHR over there. So from we were little kids and in Saint Thomas no is banana and sugar and they have plenty plantation there. We used to see because Buster would said to my to my Auntie Buster, um vie where you don't leave Buster. Where? Don't leave Manley and come to me, Buster. Auntie would say no, and she stay with the father. Manley. You see, because she was NP. But so we grew up listening to the talk and hearing and seeing what's going on. So when we come here at the early age, the politics and everything was not new for new for us to listen or to know what's going down and to have concern about we surrounding around people what it takes to make the machinery work. So we come and we get into these people here, and we still go with people in our heart and love treating everybody nice and the people them gravitate to. My husband, because although those days you could never say you are a Republican, you would have to say it way down in your throat because it was three and a half Democrats to one Republican by or they equated those days, but nevertheless, we never let anything get us down. We never make color get us down. We never make our voice get us down because we're from another area. We just go like those things is not a part of us not even being different with our pigmentation. I don't like to go into the race thing and say black and white because people are people. So you have your you are Caucasian, your skin is light, good for you. My skin is dark, good for me, and I love it and I appreciate it, and I wouldn't want it any other way. And I don't use that as a crutch not to come up the ladder or believe anybody is tapping my back. Anybody stopping me back is my own self by not going forward. Don't you feel that way? Anybody stopping you okay. And don't you love your skin? Okay. That's the same way I feel. No excuses. When did your husband first run for office? Back in the 60s. Was he the only black man doing so at the time? No. My husband was the second black man. The first black man was Clarke. Mr. Clarke, first name? No. Oh, I just forget Clarke first name. But Clarke was the first one and he was Republic. He was Democrat. Clarke was Democrat. My husband was Republican. What was his platform? Um. Or should I phrase that now? What are you looking for with this platform? When he was running for, uh, when he was running for office and he was going out there among the people, what sort of things was he saying? Well, what he was saying that he was doing. First of all, it was education. It was housing. Now that housing was bad, but housing with a different tone. He was thinking that instead of we being concentrated because we had these streets like Westhaven Street, Canton Street, Portland Street, and they had these great big apartment building like a horseshoe right around, and it was just concentrated. My husband at the time thought, which he tried to sell them the idea they was not for it, but like now they are gravitating to it. He was saying, let's build housing so that when you put the other people with the more affluent people, you would bring them along with you instead of leave them in an isolated situation. And 30 to 35 years later, that become a terrible, terrible, painful situation, as we have witnessed because we went to a nice, vibrant place, to a place more of a welfare and no reflection. But the situation was created because you have all these projects, you have Stone village, you have Charter Hook, you have right sides. And today, as I'm speaking to you, they bulldozed on all of these places and putting up beautiful single families because it was infested with people with different ills. I need not tell you what the different ills are. But it was not befitting to the community. If you take a ride now, you will see all these places. Two villages in my backyard. Because I was living at the first house in town. I know I'm nearer down to Barber Street, and Stowe Village was more to speak in my back yard one block over on Stowe Village, and now it's all been torn down. Every bit of it is grazed and they are putting up luxurious single family homes. So you'll attract a different kind of people. So all that welfare state, the money for the people with a lot of kids and the environment diminishes. Mhm. Why was it so unpopular at the time when your husband was, was. Because the power to be, did not realize what he was trying to say or didn't want to. But so as it becomes a financial burden to keep the vast amount of people living, feeding them cheap rent, maintaining the the place, it was more bringing down the state financially, the burden of so many people not doing, and all the affluent people that could manage left the state factories has gone for cheap labor elsewhere. And we have a situation, as it now is. What kind of relationship, um, existed between, you know, these people, African Americans in the Hartford area in the late 1950s and the West Indians who were coming in. We had, I would say, a good relationship as far as I've seen it. Um, the kids from West Indian are good settlement of them. The kids. The children, the offspring. Because, you know, they come and they married into the families here. I'm happy to say that a lot of them came with, very well, well-educated. You don't see or you don't hear of them, but they have done pretty good. A lot of them. Mhm. Very well. What about your children. What are they doing now. My children. To each his own. You do the best with them and you try to help them. One of my son. He was a plumber, but he took sick. He's a kidney patient now, so he can't do the hard work. And that's the first one. The second one. He has a small business that he's done landscaping and he does very well. He has a small business. And one of my son. The third boy was a fireman, did very well, but he got hurt. So now, um, he's on early retirement because he has pins in his legs. He went through a building. My daughter is fine. And, um, she got her degree in finance. Sharon and Lisa is a teacher, and Dawn is still in college, so she would like to be a lawyer. So I don't know. So that's six of them I have. And, uh. What? How do they, um. They're all born overseas in this country. Yeah. Do you think that they have a different sense of their West Indian identity than you and Mr. Bennett, since you were born in Jamaica and came here when you were? No, they don't, because I always take them at least once a year to Jamaica at least once a year, if not twice. And what was it like to to go back home after having been settled here for a little while? Going home? It's no big thing. We go home all the time. But I mean, like, how how is Jamaica changed all these over all these years? It has changed because of the government changing. Each person that comes into office, have his own agenda and come about with his own changes. Not anything for the worse, but, um, each party does their own job. They don't. It's not a continuation of you have a five year plan. And where this plan stops, somebody picked up and carry it on. Each person come and have their own agenda. So if you had a continuity of a project, then you would see you would watch it growing, coming on stream, you know. But then each person, it's like, for instance, if you're making a road from the airport to Negril, if it didn't get finished in this administration, the next administration would take it on. It tend to just stop where the last administration was. So you see what I mean? You can't see the progress. You cannot see it. You just cannot see it. And how did your mother did your mother continue to prosper despite all of these decades that have gone by? Well, my mother came to this country and she died a year ago. She lived until she was 83. So she was here when she died. Mhm. When did your mother come? My mother came here maybe about 15 years ago. Yeah. Was there a particular reason why she chose well? Well, most of the kids were here, and, um, we decided to let her come. We didn't really, you know, want her to be in the cold. But then she had freedom where she could travel up and down, you know? So we let her come. What about your father? He passed. Um. Over 16, 17 years ago. Oh, so your mother was almost. Alone, right? Because all the children were here. And what about the kind of West Indian identity that has emerged since the 1950s, when there weren't that many people here? You know, compared to the 1990s, when there were thousands and thousands of Westerners here in the area. Well, you know, early on. Our name was like gold. We would never want for anything, because if somebody want you even for housework, and if they were to call and ask you if you know someone and you said yes, I know someone, they would say, send them. They would not ask a second question. They would leave the house open. They would say, I'm going to leave the key, let her come and she'll know what to do. I'll leave the instruction, what they want to do clean the house, vacuum or whatever. And we had a very, very good name. Very, very good name. But then as time goes on, somewhere along the line that changes. And we don't have those loving, caring people anymore around. And even to get a day's work for some people who maybe don't want a full time job because they're older in age and all that, they can't find that sort of menial jobs to help out. Mark you not saying it's the best thing, but if you could go out and you could make three days a week and you would have whatever is the going rate, whereas $6,070 a day you would know you have three days and you would not have to put in a full day's a full week's pay because you're physically you're not up to it. You can't get that anymore. It's not there. What do you attribute this change to? The change because it's a global thing or it's becoming a global thing. Here in Connecticut we used to have Colts manufacturing, which is the gun people. We used to have Pratt and Whitney, which is a heavy engine for planes. And so and you would have the gold leaf people. You have a lot of industry, a lot of industry. Now they are heading south. Or if they don't head south, they're gone far away to Taiwan, different places where labor is cheap and in so doing, the vacuum is here, that there's no factories that they used to be, and therefore the people is here without any jobs, especially the people in the lower. Lower end of the pole. There's no space for them. It becomes, and I should say, in a little while will be all high tech, because if you're not sophisticated enough to be in computer and all of that stuff, there will be no space for you because there's no space with menial jobs anymore. McDonald's is here, there, and the fast food. But how many of those can take the population? That's where some of our people fits in, because we are not all educated, younger people coming in, going to college and all of that. But for the average person that does not have a high school, they find it very hard.

lines.line: # Family Roots and Childhood in Jamaica

> The speaker introduces her Jamaican heritage, mentioning her parents' origins and her own birth in Lower Saint Andrew, Jamaica, in 1935. She recalls a happy childhood as the eldest of twelve children, growing up in a home surrounded by fruit trees, flowers, and livestock. The family was self-sufficient, raising goats, fowls, and turkeys, with her mother managing the household and preparing for the Christmas market.

metadata.title: Family Roots and Childhood in Jamaica
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces her Jamaican heritage, mentioning her parents' origins and her own birth in Lower Saint Andrew, Jamaica, in 1935. She recalls a happy childhood as the eldest of twelve children, growing up in a home surrounded by fruit trees, flowers, and livestock. The family was self-sufficient, raising goats, fowls, and turkeys, with her mother managing the household and preparing for the Christmas market.

lines.line: # Migration Patterns and Family Abroad

> The speaker reflects on the migration trends in her community, noting that many people traveled to places like Liberia and England. She shares that her grand uncle moved to England in 1914, later settling in Australia, and that she herself went to England in 1952 to pursue a nursing career, staying with her uncle who worked at the Pentonville Post Office. The family's history of travel and migration is highlighted, as well as the opportunities available for those with the means to go abroad.

She shares that her grand uncle moved to England in 1914, later settling in Australia, and that she herself went to England in 1952 to pursue a nursing career, staying with her uncle who worked at the Pentonville Post Office. The family's history of travel and migration is highlighted, as well as the opportunities available for those with the means to go abroad.

lines.line: # Experiences as a Young Jamaican Woman in England

> The speaker recounts her five years in England, including witnessing the Queen's coronation and enjoying London's vibrant social scene. She describes her positive experiences as a student nurse, noting the kindness of patients and colleagues despite being called 'darky,' which she interpreted as affectionate. She also discusses the rationing system, the challenges of adapting to British customs, and the supportive environment she found among both staff and patients.

She also discusses the rationing system, the challenges of adapting to British customs, and the supportive environment she found among both staff and patients.

lines.line: # Migration to America and Early Years

> The speaker explains her move to the United States, following her husband who had arrived earlier as a farm worker due to restrictive immigration quotas. She describes their childhood friendship in Jamaica, their correspondence while apart, and her eventual decision to join him in America after completing her nursing training in England. The narrative touches on the challenges of immigration, family expectations, and the sacrifices made to build a new life together.

She describes their childhood friendship in Jamaica, their correspondence while apart, and her eventual decision to join him in America after completing her nursing training in England. The narrative touches on the challenges of immigration, family expectations, and the sacrifices made to build a new life together.

lines.line: # Settling in Hartford and Overcoming Housing Discrimination

> The speaker discusses the process of settling in Hartford, Connecticut, where her husband faced racial discrimination when trying to rent an apartment. With the help of a lawyer friend, they purchased a dilapidated two-family house on Elmer Street, which they renovated extensively. She describes the hard work involved in maintaining the home, including cleaning the coal furnace, and their resourcefulness in using materials from the local dump to build a fence, reflecting their determination and skills in home improvement.

metadata.title: Settling in Hartford and Overcoming Housing Discrimination
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the process of settling in Hartford, Connecticut, where her husband faced racial discrimination when trying to rent an apartment. With the help of a lawyer friend, they purchased a dilapidated two-family house on Elmer Street, which they renovated extensively. She describes the hard work involved in maintaining the home, including cleaning the coal furnace, and their resourcefulness in using materials from the local dump to build a fence, reflecting their determination and skills in home improvement.

lines.line: # Early Married Life and Social Norms in Hartford

> The speaker describes the strict social norms of the 1950s, which prohibited unmarried couples from living together under threat of arrest. She and her husband lived separately—she on Enfield Street and he on Clark Street—until they were married and could move in together. She also recounts their move to a larger home on Tower Avenue as their family grew, highlighting the challenges and transitions of early married life in a new country.

metadata.title: Early Married Life and Social Norms in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker describes the strict social norms of the 1950s, which prohibited unmarried couples from living together under threat of arrest. She and her husband lived separately—she on Enfield Street and he on Clark Street—until they were married and could move in together. She also recounts their move to a larger home on Tower Avenue as their family grew, highlighting the challenges and transitions of early married life in a new country.

lines.line: # Contrasts Between Life in London and Hartford

> The speaker compares her experiences in London and Hartford, noting differences in amenities such as refrigeration and bathing facilities. In London, daily life involved buying fresh food from local vendors and using public bathhouses, while Hartford offered more modern conveniences similar to Jamaica. She also describes the central location of her London residence and the cultural adjustments required when moving between these cities.

metadata.title: Contrasts Between Life in London and Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker compares her experiences in London and Hartford, noting differences in amenities such as refrigeration and bathing facilities. In London, daily life involved buying fresh food from local vendors and using public bathhouses, while Hartford offered more modern conveniences similar to Jamaica. She also describes the central location of her London residence and the cultural adjustments required when moving between these cities.

lines.line: # West Indian Community and Businesses in Hartford

> The speaker discusses the small, close-knit West Indian community in Hartford during her early years there, contrasting it with the larger community in London. She describes the excitement of welcoming new Jamaicans, the lack of West Indian businesses initially, and the challenges of sourcing traditional foods, which often had to be brought from New York. Over time, entrepreneurs like Mr. Leslie established stores catering to the community's needs, and local individuals began making traditional foods such as patties.

metadata.title: West Indian Community and Businesses in Hartford
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the small, close-knit West Indian community in Hartford during her early years there, contrasting it with the larger community in London. She describes the excitement of welcoming new Jamaicans, the lack of West Indian businesses initially, and the challenges of sourcing traditional foods, which often had to be brought from New York. Over time, entrepreneurs like Mr. Leslie established stores catering to the community's needs, and local individuals began making traditional foods such as patties.

lines.line: # Political Involvement and Community Leadership

> The speaker outlines her husband's political career in Hartford, where he served as a Republican city council member for four terms. She describes their family's political background in Jamaica, including connections to prominent leaders like Bustamante and Manley, and the influence of political discussions during her upbringing. Her husband advocated for integrated housing and education, ideas that were initially unpopular but later recognized as forward-thinking. The speaker emphasizes their resilience in the face of racial and political challenges, and their commitment to community improvement.

metadata.title: Political Involvement and Community Leadership

She describes their family's political background in Jamaica, including connections to prominent leaders like Bustamante and Manley, and the influence of political discussions during her upbringing. Her husband advocated for integrated housing and education, ideas that were initially unpopular but later recognized as forward-thinking. The speaker emphasizes their resilience in the face of racial and political challenges, and their commitment to community improvement.

lines.line: # Relations Between African Americans and West Indians

> The speaker reflects on the generally positive relationships between African Americans and West Indians in Hartford during the late 1950s. She notes that many West Indian immigrants and their children integrated well, often marrying into local families and achieving educational and professional success. The speaker expresses pride in the accomplishments of the West Indian community and the harmonious interactions with African Americans.

metadata.title: Relations Between African Americans and West Indians
metadata.synopsis: The speaker reflects on the generally positive relationships between African Americans and West Indians in Hartford during the late 1950s. She notes that many West Indian immigrants and their children integrated well, often marrying into local families and achieving educational and professional success. The speaker expresses pride in the accomplishments of the West Indian community and the harmonious interactions with African Americans.

lines.line: # Family, Children, and Preserving Heritage

> The speaker shares details about her six children, their careers, and health challenges. She emphasizes her efforts to maintain their connection to their Jamaican heritage by taking them to Jamaica at least once a year. The children, all born in the United States, are encouraged to embrace their West Indian identity, and the speaker expresses satisfaction with their achievements and cultural ties.

metadata.title: Family, Children, and Preserving Heritage

She emphasizes her efforts to maintain their connection to their Jamaican heritage by taking them to Jamaica at least once a year. The children, all born in the United States, are encouraged to embrace their West Indian identity, and the speaker expresses satisfaction with their achievements and cultural ties.

lines.line: # Changes in Jamaica and Family Migration to the US

> The speaker discusses the changes she observed in Jamaica over the years, attributing them to shifts in government and a lack of continuity in development projects. She recounts her mother's migration to the United States to join her children and the reasons for bringing her over, including family unity and the freedom to travel. The speaker also mentions her father's earlier passing and the impact of family migration on their lives.

metadata.title: Changes in Jamaica and Family Migration to the US
metadata.synopsis: The speaker discusses the changes she observed in Jamaica over the years, attributing them to shifts in government and a lack of continuity in development projects. She recounts her mother's migration to the United States to join her children and the reasons for bringing her over, including family unity and the freedom to travel. The speaker also mentions her father's earlier passing and the impact of family migration on their lives.

lines.line: # Changing Reputation and Economic Challenges for West Indians

> The speaker reflects on the evolution of the West Indian community's reputation in Hartford, noting that they were once highly trusted and sought after for work. Over time, this reputation changed, and opportunities for menial jobs diminished due to the decline of local industries and the outsourcing of manufacturing jobs. She observes that the shift toward a high-tech economy has made it increasingly difficult for less-educated individuals to find employment, impacting the community's economic stability and social cohesion.

story.title: Molly Bennett Part 1
story.description: The transcript recounts an interview with Mrs. Molly Bennett, focusing on her family's journey from Jamaica to the West Indies and later to the United States. Born in 1935 in Jamaica, Bennett shares her experiences growing up in a self-sustaining family environment. She traveled to England in 1952 for nursing education, witnessing the Queen's coronation. In 1957, she joined her husband in Hartford, USA, where they navigated the challenges of racial discrimination and housing issues. Her husband's political career included efforts to enhance education and housing policies. The narrative also touches on the evolving West Indian community in Hartford and the socio-economic changes impacting employment opportunities over the years.
